name: r-development
description: Modern R development practices emphasizing tidyverse patterns (dplyr 1.1 and later, native pipe, join_by, .by grouping), rlang metaprogramming, performance optimization, and package development. Use when Claude needs to write R code, create R packages, optimize R performance, or provide R programming guidance.
capabilities: Guide tidyverse patterns using dplyr 1.1+ features like native pipe and join_by
Implement rlang data-masking and metaprogramming with {{ }} and !! operators
Optimize R performance with profiling tools like profvis and bench
Create R packages following modern best practices and structure
Use modern object systems including S3, S4, and S7
Apply performance optimization strategies for large data operations
author: CodingKaiser
securitySummary: Pure documentation skill containing only markdown files with R code examples. No executable code, network calls, filesystem access, or external commands. All capabilities align with stated purpose. Static scanner flagged patterns in documentation text as security issues, but all are false positives.
valueStatement: Writing R code with outdated patterns wastes time and creates technical debt. This skill provides current best practices for tidyverse, rlang metaprogramming, and package development so you can write clean, efficient R code.
userTitle: Write modern R code with tidyverse patterns
limitations: Does not execute R code or install packages
Does not access local R environments or libraries
Does not provide runtime debugging or error fixing
Focused on best practices, not introductory R concepts

useCases.title: Modernize data pipelines
useCases.description: Upgrade legacy R code to current tidyverse patterns for cleaner, faster data pipelines.

useCases.title: Build quality packages
useCases.description: Create maintainable R packages with proper structure, testing, and documentation.

useCases.title: Optimize slow code
useCases.description: Identify and fix performance bottlenecks using profiling and benchmarking tools.

promptTemplates.title: Filter data
promptTemplates.prompt: Show me how to filter rows in R using modern dplyr with multiple conditions and .by grouping.

promptTemplates.title: Join tables
promptTemplates.prompt: Write an inner join using join_by() with inequality matching and multiple match handling.

promptTemplates.title: Create function
promptTemplates.prompt: Create an R function that uses rlang {{ }} syntax to accept column names as arguments.

promptTemplates.title: Build package
promptTemplates.prompt: Set up a complete R package structure with DESCRIPTION, NAMESPACE, testing, and proper documentation patterns.

bestPractices: Use native pipe |> instead of magrittr %>% for modern R 4.1+
Profile code with profvis before optimizing to find real bottlenecks
Validate inputs in user-facing functions, assume valid in internal helpers
antiPatterns: Avoid group_by() |> summarise() |> ungroup() pattern, use .by instead
Do not use library() in packages, use Imports in DESCRIPTION
Avoid growing vectors in loops, pre-allocate instead

faq.question: Which R version is required for modern tidyverse patterns?
faq.answer: R 4.1+ is required for native pipe |>. dplyr 1.1+ required for join_by() and .by.

faq.question: What is the difference between {{}} and .data[[]]?
faq.answer: {{}} forwards column arguments to data-masking functions. .data[[]] accesses columns by name as strings.

faq.question: How do I integrate this skill with existing R projects?
faq.answer: Reference the skill when writing R code or need guidance. It provides patterns to apply in your existing codebase.

faq.question: Does this skill store or share my data?
faq.answer: No. This is a read-only documentation skill with no file, network, or data access capabilities.

faq.question: How is this different from R documentation websites?
faq.answer: This skill curates best practices specifically for AI assistants, with focused examples and actionable guidance.

faq.question: Should I use S3 or S7 for new projects?
faq.answer: Use S7 for new projects needing validation and structure. S3 remains good for simple classes and maximum compatibility.
